The timestamp after which the cached result is considered stale and should no longer be reused, ensuring workflows rely on fresh data when needed.

tables.description: Maintains a registry of trusted publishers whose cryptographic identities are recognized by the system for verifying the authenticity and integrity of signed content. The registry supports the full lifecycle of a publisher relationship, from initial registration through active use to formal revocation when trust is no longer warranted.

columns.description: Unique internal identifier assigned to each trusted publisher record for system reference and tracking.
The human-readable name of the publisher as it should appear throughout the platform and in any published content.
The cryptographic public key used to verify digital signatures and confirm that content was genuinely issued by this publisher.
A short, easily recognizable hash derived from the public key, used to quickly confirm which specific key is associated with a publisher.
Indicates whether the publisher is currently permitted to sign and submit content, allowing administrators to temporarily suspend trust without deleting the record.
Free-form text providing additional context about the publisher, such as their role, ownership, or the nature of content they produce.
The official web address for the publisher, allowing users and auditors to learn more about the organization behind the content.
The username or identifier of the person who originally registered the publisher in the system.
The date and time when the publisher was first added to the trusted registry.
The username or identifier of the person who most recently modified the publisher's record.
The date and time of the most recent change made to the publisher's record.
A written explanation of why the publisher's trusted status was withdrawn, providing an audit trail of the decision.
The date and time when the publisher's trusted status was formally revoked, after which their content should no longer be accepted.

tables.description: This table manages the membership relationships between individual users and tenant workspaces in a multi-tenant platform. It tracks which users belong to which tenants, their roles and invitation status, and enables users to be associated with multiple tenants while designating a default workspace. This membership structure is foundational for organizing users, enforcing access control, and delivering tenant-scoped experiences across the platform.

columns.description: Unique identifier for each user-tenant membership record, distinguishing every individual association between a user and a tenant workspace.
Reference to the authenticated user account in the identity provider, linking this membership record to the person's core login identity across the platform.
Identifies the specific tenant workspace to which the user has been granted access, enabling organization and isolation of data and resources per tenant.
Defines the user's permission level within the tenant, such as administrator, member, or viewer, governing what actions and resources they can access within that workspace.
The contact email address associated with the user for this particular tenant membership, which may differ from other tenants they belong to.
The human-readable name shown for the user within the tenant's interface, allowing administrators and team members to identify the person in conversations, assignments, and activity logs.

Indicates whether this tenant is the user's primary or preferred workspace that loads by default when they sign in, helping users with multiple tenant memberships navigate quickly to their main environment.
Tracks the current state of the user's onboarding to the tenant, such as pending, accepted, or declined, allowing administrators to manage who has been invited versus who has actively joined.
Records the date and time when the user formally became an active member of the tenant, marking the start of their access and participation in that workspace.
Captures the date and time when this membership record was first created in the system, providing an audit trail of when the relationship was established.
Reflects the most recent date and time when any details of this membership record were modified, such as role changes, invitation status updates, or default tenant reassignments.

tables.description: Tracks which users belong to which workspaces and defines their access level within each. This enables multi-tenant collaboration by managing membership, assigned roles, and the history of who was added by whom across the platform.

columns.description: Unique identifier for each user-workspace membership record in the system.
Identifies the individual user from the platform's identity management system who is being granted workspace access.
Specifies which workspace the user has been granted membership in.
Defines the user's permission level and capabilities within that particular workspace, such as admin, member, or viewer.
Records the date and time when the user became a member of the workspace.
Identifies the existing user or administrator who invited or assigned this person to the workspace.
Captures when the membership record was originally established in the system.
Tracks the most recent change made to the user's workspace membership details, such as role updates or reassignments.

tables.description: Stores snapshots of AI agent workflow executions when they are temporarily paused, capturing the full state needed to seamlessly resume processing later. Each checkpoint represents a saved point in an agent's decision journey, enabling workflows to be interrupted—typically for human review or external input—and continued without losing progress. This supports reliable long-running agent operations across tenants, projects, and workspaces.

columns.description: Unique identifier for each workflow checkpoint record.
References the specific run of a workflow that this checkpoint belongs to.
Identifies the workflow definition that was being executed when the checkpoint was created.
The customer organization that owns this checkpoint, used to isolate data between tenants.
The project grouping this checkpoint belongs to, allowing related workflows to be organized together.
The workspace environment where the workflow execution was checkpointed.
Identifies the specific step or node within the workflow where execution was paused.
Indicates the node from which the workflow should continue once it is resumed.
Captures the complete state of variables, inputs, and intermediate results at the moment of suspension so execution can continue seamlessly.
Stores the structured representation of the workflow at checkpoint time, preserving the blueprint for resumption.
The current lifecycle state of the checkpoint, such as whether it is active, expired, or has been consumed.
Explains why the workflow execution was paused, such as awaiting human approval, external input, or a scheduled delay.
Links to the AI agent request that triggered or was involved in the checkpointed workflow execution.
The date and time after which the checkpoint is no longer valid and cannot be used to resume the workflow.
Indicates whether the workflow should fail entirely or continue with errors encountered after resuming from this checkpoint.
The date and time when the checkpoint was originally created during workflow suspension.
The date and time when the checkpoint record was most recently modified.

tables.description: This table records the execution history of automated workflows, capturing when they ran, how long they took, and whether they succeeded or failed. It supports operational monitoring, troubleshooting, and audit compliance across different tenants and workspaces. Each entry provides a complete trace of a single workflow invocation, including the inputs processed and any errors encountered.

columns.description: Unique identifier assigned to each workflow execution record, used to distinguish and reference individual runs across the system.
Reference to the specific workflow definition that was executed, linking the log entry to the automated process that ran.
Shared identifier that ties related operations and logs together, enabling end-to-end tracing of a request or business process across multiple systems.
The date and time when the workflow execution began, used to track scheduling, latency, and operational timelines.
The date and time when the workflow execution finished, whether successfully or with an error, used to calculate total runtime.
The outcome of the workflow execution, such as success, failure, or in-progress, used for monitoring and alerting.
The data and parameters provided to the workflow when it was triggered, preserved for auditing, debugging, and replay purposes.
Human-readable description of any failure that occurred during execution, helping support teams and developers diagnose issues.
Total runtime of the workflow execution in milliseconds, used to monitor performance, identify bottlenecks, and track service-level objectives.
Number of days the execution record should be retained before automatic cleanup, supporting data retention and compliance policies.
The date and time when the execution log entry was first written to the system, establishing an audit trail.
The most recent date and time when the execution record was modified, used to track changes to status or related fields.
Flag indicating whether the execution was run in developer mode for testing or experimentation purposes rather than production use.
Identifies the workspace or team environment associated with the workflow execution, supporting organizational separation and access control.
Identifies the customer organization that owns the workflow execution, enabling multi-tenant isolation and per-customer reporting.

tables.description: This table defines the conditions and configurations that initiate automated workflows within the platform. Each trigger represents a specific entry point—such as an incoming API call, scheduled event, or system signal—that activates a particular workflow for a given project and tenant. It governs how external requests or internal events are received, validated, and routed to the appropriate workflow execution.

columns.description: A unique identifier assigned to each trigger entry, used to reference and manage a specific workflow activation point.
References the specific workflow that will be launched when this trigger fires, linking the trigger to its corresponding automated process.
Identifies the project under which this trigger operates, allowing triggers to be grouped and managed at the project level.
Indicates which customer organization owns this trigger, ensuring triggers are isolated and scoped to the correct tenant environment.
Defines the kind of event or condition that will activate the workflow, such as a webhook call, schedule, or platform event.
A human-readable label given to the trigger to make it easy to identify and manage within the user interface.
A short summary explaining the trigger's purpose and the scenario it is designed to handle.
Indicates whether the trigger is currently enabled and will activate workflows, or disabled and ignored.
The HTTP action (such as GET, POST, or PUT) that the trigger accepts when it is invoked as a web endpoint.
The format of the request body that the trigger expects, for example JSON or form-encoded data.
The largest allowable size of an incoming request payload for this trigger, protecting the platform from oversized submissions.
The maximum number of times the trigger can be invoked within a defined time window, preventing abuse and overuse.
The date and time when the trigger was originally created and added to the system.
The date and time when the trigger's configuration was most recently modified.
Categorizes the origin of the trigger, helping distinguish between user-defined triggers and those generated by the platform or integrations.

tables.description: This table stores automated workflow definitions that orchestrate business processes and AI-driven tasks. Each workflow represents a configurable sequence of operations triggered by events, schedules, or manual actions, and is organized within a tenant's workspace and project structure. The table supports versioning and lifecycle management, enabling teams to design, deploy, and evolve complex automation flows.

columns.description: A unique identifier that distinguishes each workflow from all others in the system.
The human-readable label assigned to the workflow for easy identification by team members.
The complete configuration and structural definition of the workflow, including its steps, logic, and execution settings.
The date and time when the workflow was first authored and saved.
The date and time when the workflow was most recently modified.
A brief narrative explaining the purpose and intended outcome of the workflow.
The category of mechanism that initiates the workflow, such as manual invocation, scheduled execution, or event-driven activation.
The originating system, channel, or integration that produces the event causing the workflow to start.
The specific kind of event or signal that the workflow listens for in order to begin executing.
The current lifecycle state of the workflow, such as active, paused, draft, or archived.
The total number of individual steps, actions, or processing blocks contained within the workflow.
The total number of connections that link workflow steps together and define the execution path.
The conditional expression that determines whether an incoming trigger event should activate the workflow.
The project grouping to which the workflow belongs, supporting logical organization within a workspace.
The owning organization or customer account that has authority over the workflow.
The release label identifying the iteration of the workflow definition, supporting change tracking and rollback.
The collaborative workspace environment where the workflow is created, managed, and shared.

tables.description: Workspaces represent distinct working environments or organizational units within a tenant account, allowing customers to separate projects, teams, or business functions under a single account. Each workspace has its own branding, settings, and operational status, enabling flexible structuring of work and resources across the platform.

columns.description: Unique internal identifier assigned to each workspace record in the system.
Business-facing identifier used to reference a specific workspace in integrations, URLs, and external communications.
Identifier linking the workspace to its owning tenant or customer organization, ensuring workspaces are properly scoped within their parent account.
The human-readable display name of the workspace as it appears to users throughout the platform.
A short, URL-friendly version of the workspace name used in web addresses and links to ensure clean, readable routing.
Free-form text describing the purpose, scope, or intended use of the workspace for team members.
The current operational state of the workspace, indicating whether it is active, archived, suspended, or in another condition.
Flag indicating whether this workspace is the default landing destination for users belonging to the tenant.
A flexible collection of configurable preferences and options that govern how the workspace behaves and what features are enabled.
A visual icon or symbol used to represent and quickly identify the workspace in navigation and listings.
A color theme applied to the workspace's icon and visual elements to aid visual distinction between workspaces.
The user who originally set up and created the workspace.
The user who most recently made changes to the workspace's information or configuration.
The date and time when the workspace was initially created.
The date and time when the workspace's information or settings were most recently modified.
